1
Property Issue Summary
The property inspection of Flat No. 103 (and associated areas) revealed widespread dampness and leakage across all major rooms — Hall, Bedrooms, Kitchen, Parking, and Common Bathrooms — primarily driven by systemic failure of bathroom tile grout joints and concealed plumbing. Thermal imaging confirmed active moisture retention across 30 readings, with all readings showing ΔT ~5°C signalling persistent moisture. The most critical finding is active slab leakage into the parking ceiling below, requiring immediate structural intervention.
⏰ Repair Urgency: Immediate (within 48hrs) for parking slab; Short-term (2 weeks) for bathroom waterproofing
2
Area-wise Observations
Hall — Flat No. 103
High
Conf: 88%
⬇ Negative Side (Affected Area)
Observed dampness at the skirting level of the Hall of Flat No. 103. The bottom 15–20cm of walls shows visible moisture staining and salt efflorescence deposits.
⬆ Positive Side (Source)
Gaps observed between tile joints of the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103, allowing water to seep through to the hall walls below.
Evidence Strength
88%
INSPECTION evidence · 88% conf
THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause
Failure of grout in Common Bathroom tile joints allowing water to percolate laterally and vertically into hall skirting walls.
Severity Basis
Skirting dampness covering the entire hall perimeter indicates widespread water ingress from the bathroom above. Efflorescence confirms prolonged exposure.
Actions
  • Re-grout all Common Bathroom tile joints with epoxy-based waterproof grout
  • Apply crystalline waterproofing slurry to hall skirting walls (internal surface)
  • Inspect and repair Nahani trap for any misalignment or cracks
  • Apply anti-dampness paint on hall skirting after drying
🧠 AI Reasoning Trace
Inspection shows skirting dampness → source identified as bathroom tile gaps → thermal confirms moisture retention in walls → HIGH severity due to extent

Tile joint gaps in Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103 are the common source for both hall and bedroom dampness, indicating widespread grout failure.

85%

THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause

Severity Basis
Same bathroom affecting two rooms confirms large-scale waterproofing failure — not isolated to one joint.
Actions
  • Priority: Fix Common Bathroom grout as this resolves both hall and bedroom issues
  • Apply waterproof membrane on bedroom skirting wall (negative side treatment)
  • Hack and re-plaster affected skirting area after waterproofing

Master Bedroom — Flat No. 103
High
Conf: 90%
⬇ Negative Side (Affected Area)
Dampness and efflorescence observed both at skirting level and on the wall surface of the Master Bedroom of Flat No. 103. Efflorescence (salt deposits) indicates long-term chronic moisture exposure.
⬆ Positive Side (Source)
Tile joint gaps in Master Bedroom Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Possible secondary contribution from external wall crack near the bedroom.
Evidence Strength
90%
INSPECTION evidence · 90% conf
THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause
Primary: Master Bedroom Bathroom tile joint failure. Secondary: External wall crack near duct providing rainwater ingress. Both pathways converge in the master bedroom wall.
Severity Basis
Efflorescence on wall surface (not just skirting) signals chronic long-term moisture — the problem has been present for months or more. Dual sources (bathroom + external) increase severity.
Actions
  • Immediate: Seal external wall cracks with elastomeric crack filler
  • Re-grout Master Bedroom Bathroom floor and wall tiles completely
  • Remove efflorescence chemically and apply crystalline waterproofing coat
  • Repaint with anti-fungal, breathable paint after drying
🧠 AI Reasoning Trace
Efflorescence = chronic exposure → dual sources confirmed → higher confidence due to physical evidence of salt deposits

External Wall — Near Master Bedroom, Flat No. 103
High
Conf: 87%
⬇ Negative Side (Affected Area)
Damp wall surface and efflorescence on the exterior-facing Master Bedroom wall, consistent with rainwater ingress through external wall defects.
⬆ Positive Side (Source)
Moderate cracks observed on external wall surface near Master Bedroom duct. Gaps around duct/pipe penetrations creating direct rainwater ingress pathways.
Evidence Strength
87%
INSPECTION evidence · 87% conf
THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause
Moderate cracks and unsealed duct penetrations on the external wall allow rainwater ingress. This combines with internal bathroom leakage to saturate the master bedroom wall.
Severity Basis
External cracks and open duct gaps create a direct path for monsoon rainwater into the building fabric. Combined with internal bathroom leakage, the wall is under attack from both sides.
Actions
  • Seal all external wall cracks with elastomeric crack filler (PU-based)
  • Apply 2 coats elastomeric waterproof external paint (weather-coat)
  • Seal all duct/pipe penetrations with polyurethane sealant
  • Inspect and repair external plumbing pipe openings
🧠 AI Reasoning Trace
External + internal dual attack confirmed → moderate crack = clear ingress path → HIGH severity due to year-round rainwater risk

Parking Ceiling — Below Flat No. 103
Critical
Conf: 95%
⬇ Negative Side (Affected Area)
Active leakage observed on the parking area ceiling directly below Flat No. 103. Water marks, staining and dripping visible on the RCC slab ceiling.
⬆ Positive Side (Source)
Concealed plumbing leakage and open tile joints in Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103 above. Water has accumulated sufficiently to penetrate the full RCC slab.
Evidence Strength
95%
INSPECTION evidence · 95% conf
THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause
Complete failure of the waterproofing membrane (brickbat coba layer) under the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Concealed plumbing leakage adds to water load. Water has pooled sufficiently to breach the structural slab.
Severity Basis
CRITICAL — Active water penetrating a structural RCC slab is a serious structural concern. It indicates the waterproofing layer under the bathroom has completely failed. Parking areas are occupied by vehicles and people — safety risk.
Actions
  • IMMEDIATE: Engage structural engineer to assess RCC slab integrity
  • IMMEDIATE: Isolate parking area below until slab integrity confirmed
  • Fix all plumbing joint leakages in Flat No. 103 Common Bathroom
  • Break and re-lay bathroom floor with new waterproof membrane (APP/SBS)
  • Apply epoxy injection or crystalline waterproofing on parking slab from below
  • Install stainless steel drip channels on parking ceiling to redirect residual water
🧠 AI Reasoning Trace
Active slab leakage = water has breached structural element → highest evidence quality → CRITICAL with 0.95 confidence

Common Bathroom Ceiling — Flat No. 103
Moderate
Conf: 78%
⬇ Negative Side (Affected Area)
Mild dampness observed on the ceiling of the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Ceiling shows moisture staining and mild seepage marks.
⬆ Positive Side (Source)
Open tile joints and outlet leakage in both Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms of Flat No. 203 (floor above). Water from Flat 203 is seeping through the floor slab into Flat 103's bathroom ceiling.
Evidence Strength
78%
INSPECTION evidence · 78% conf
THERMAL evidence · 95% conf
Root Cause
Open tile joints and outlet leakage in bathrooms of Flat No. 203 (above) allowing water to seep through the floor slab into the ceiling of Flat No. 103.
Severity Basis
Moderate severity — mild dampness from above-flat source. Requires coordination between two separate flat owners. Not immediately structural but will worsen if Flat 203 is not rectified.
Actions
  • Formally notify Flat No. 203 owner/building management of the issue
  • Re-grout all tile joints in Flat 203 Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms
  • Repair outlet leakage in Flat 203
  • Apply anti-damp paint on Flat 103 Common Bathroom ceiling after repairs above are complete
🧠 AI Reasoning Trace
Source is in different flat → coordination dependency → lower confidence (0.78) as access may not be available → Moderate severity

3
Probable Root Causes
  • Systemic failure of tile grout joints in Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms of Flat No. 103 — open joints allow water to percolate to all adjacent areas.
  • Damaged or degraded Nahani trap and brickbat coba waterproofing layer under bathroom tiles — original waterproofing membrane has failed.
  • Concealed plumbing joint leakages in bathroom pipes of Flat No. 103 — contributing to water load in walls and slab.
  • External wall cracks and unsealed duct penetrations near Master Bedroom — direct rainwater ingress channel.
  • Open tile joints and outlet leakage in Flat No. 203 (above) — causing cross-flat seepage into Flat No. 103 bathroom ceiling.
  • Absence of functional waterproof membrane under wet areas — indicates either original construction quality issue or age-related degradation.

  • 🟠 SHORT-TERM (2 weeks): Complete re-grouting of all bathroom tiles using epoxy waterproof grout
  • 🟠 SHORT-TERM: Seal all external wall cracks with elastomeric sealant and apply weather-coat paint

Anomalies Flagged
GTC 400 C Professional
Device
27/09/22
Capture Date
Thermal inspection was conducted on 27/09/22 using a GTC 400 C Professional (Serial: 02700034772). A total of 30 thermal readings were captured across the property. Surface temperatures ranged from 20.1°C (coldspot) to 28.8°C (hotspot). The average temperature differential (ΔT) across all readings was 5.01°C, with a peak ΔT of 5.4°C recorded at Reading #1. A total of 30 readings were flagged as anomalous based on elevated temperature differentials (ΔT > 3°C threshold), strongly corroborating the inspection findings of active moisture ingress across the property.

Additional Notes
  • No previous structural audit or repair work documented — this is a first inspection.
  • Emissivity set to 0.94 for all thermal readings (appropriate for painted plaster surfaces).
  • Leakage confirmed to occur at all times — not limited to monsoon season.
  • Leakage from Flat No. 203 (above) is affecting Flat No. 103 ceiling — cross-flat coordination required.
  • Concealed plumbing leakage confirmed via inspection checklist — likely contributing to all skirting dampness.
  • External wall shows moderate cracking near Master Bedroom duct — weather ingress pathway confirmed.
